text: The video shows an inflamed appendix with a fecolith, located behind a small loop of the ileum adhered to the lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon uses a right hypochondrium grasper to stretch the ileum near the fat pad, ensuring the serosa remains undamaged. A harmonic device with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the spermatic vessel is used to perform adhesiolysis, separating the intestinal loop from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully stretches the peritoneum to perform adhesiolysis, ensuring to stay close to the peritoneum to prevent collateral thermal spread to the intestinal loop. The spermatic vessels are visible below the surgical field.

The surgeon has separated the intestinal loop, allowing easier access to the appendix. The right hypochondrium port holds the appendix, applying anterosuperior traction to reveal its base. Adhesions at the base are visible and are being dissected using a harmonic device, with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the cecum to prevent vibration transfer.

The surgeon identifies the appendicular vessel, preparing to separate it. The dissection progresses towards the base of the appendix to ensure a complete appendectomy, with a knot tied close to the cecum to prevent incomplete removal, especially in cases with fecolith.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum, which was adhered to the lateral pelvic wall, performing adhesiolysis to access the base of the appendix. Medial traction is applied to expose the peritoneum, and the folds are cut using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon has reached the base of the appendix, which is now free from adhesions. The genia coli is visible. A Mishra's knot is being applied using a grasper to catch the appendix and transfer the loop. The knot pusher is positioned to tighten the knot securely near the cecum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the appendix, ensuring the concavity of the instrument faces the appendix. The harmonic's heat burns the mucosa and disinfects any fecal matter present. An 18mm diameter structure is tightened multiple times before cutting the suture with scissors.

The surgeon carefully uses the harmonic scalpel to separate the appendix from its base, ensuring the teflon jaw is positioned upwards to avoid contact with the suture. Anterior traction is applied to prevent the harmonic from touching the knot, which could cut it, and care is taken to avoid the tip of the harmonic touching the cecum to prevent puncture.

The surgeon has successfully separated the appendix from its base. The appendix is carefully placed into a commercially available endo bag, known as an endo catch, to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity. The endo bag is then inserted through a ten millimeter port.

The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo catch bag, which has a self-retaining metal ring, and carefully drops the appendix inside. The appendix is positioned securely within the bag using a grasper, ensuring it does not fall out. The persisting suture is pulled to close the bag, and the endo catch is then removed from the surgical site.

The surgeon uses an artery forceps to assist in removing the endo bag through the stretched ten millimeter port. The wound is then closed using suture passers with number one Vicryl to prevent hernia formation due to the stretching caused by the endo bag.
The surgeon applies betadine over the base of the appendix to disinfect the area, ensuring excess betadine is removed by suction to prevent contamination.
